event: Prehistoric communities at Ban Chiang in the northeast develop one of the earliest known bronze-working and rice-farming cultures in Southeast Asia.
Iron Age communities across present-day Thailand expand metallurgy, agriculture, and long-distance trade networks.
Indianized maritime trading polities emerge in the region, adopting Indian scripts, Hindu-Buddhist religion, and statecraft.
The Mon Dvaravati civilization flourishes in the Chao Phraya basin, spreading Theravada Buddhism and Indian-influenced art.
The Hindu-Buddhist Srivijaya empire extends influence over the southern Thai peninsula and its trade routes.
The Khmer Empire rises at Angkor and gradually dominates much of central and northeastern Thailand.
Thai chieftains overthrow Khmer overlords and found the Kingdom of Sukhothai, often regarded as the first Thai state.
King Ramkhamhaeng of Sukhothai is traditionally credited with creating the Thai alphabet.
King Mangrai founds Chiang Mai as the capital of the northern Lanna kingdom.
King Ramathibodi I founds the Kingdom of Ayutthaya, which becomes a dominant power in Southeast Asia.
Ayutthaya sacks the Khmer capital of Angkor, accelerating the decline of the Khmer Empire.
Ayutthaya absorbs the weakening Sukhothai kingdom into its expanding realm.
Portuguese envoys arrive at Ayutthaya, beginning sustained European contact and trade with Siam.
Ayutthaya falls to the Burmese Toungoo dynasty, briefly becoming a vassal before reasserting independence.
King Naresuan declares Ayutthaya's independence from Burmese control and revives the kingdom's strength.
King Naresuan defeats the Burmese crown prince in a famous elephant duel, securing Siamese sovereignty.
King Narai ascends the throne and opens Ayutthaya to extensive trade and diplomacy with France and other powers.
The Siamese Revolution of 1688 expels French influence and ushers in a more isolationist period.
Burmese armies sack and destroy Ayutthaya, ending the kingdom after more than four centuries.
General Taksin reunifies the country and establishes a new capital at Thonburi.
Rama I founds the Chakri dynasty and establishes Bangkok (Rattanakosin) as the new capital.
King Mongkut (Rama IV) begins modernizing reforms and expands engagement with Western powers.
Siam signs the Bowring Treaty with Britain, opening the economy to free trade and Western commerce.
King Chulalongkorn (Rama V) begins a reign of sweeping modernization of administration, infrastructure, and law.
The Franco-Siamese crisis forces Siam to cede Laotian territories to France, beginning major losses to colonial powers.
King Chulalongkorn abolishes slavery in Siam after decades of gradual reform.
The Anglo-Siamese Treaty cedes northern Malay states to Britain, fixing much of Siam's southern border.
Siam joins the Allies in World War I, sending an expeditionary force to Europe.
A bloodless revolution ends absolute monarchy and establishes a constitutional monarchy in Siam.
The country officially changes its name from Siam to Thailand under nationalist leader Plaek Phibunsongkhram.
Japan invades Thailand during World War II, and the government allies with Japan after brief resistance.
King Ananda Mahidol dies and Bhumibol Adulyadej (Rama IX) ascends the throne; Thailand joins the United Nations.
A military coup returns the army to power, beginning decades of frequent coups and military influence.
Thailand becomes a founding member of the Southeast Asia Treaty Organization (SEATO), aligning with the West.
Thailand co-founds the Association of Southeast Asian Nations (ASEAN) in Bangkok.
A popular student-led uprising overthrows the military dictatorship and briefly restores democratic rule.
The Thammasat University massacre crushes student protesters and returns the military to power.
Black May protests against military rule end in violence, prompting King Bhumibol's intervention and renewed democratization.
The Asian financial crisis erupts from Thailand's currency collapse, devastating the economy and the wider region.
Telecommunications tycoon Thaksin Shinawatra wins a landslide election, reshaping Thai politics.
The Indian Ocean tsunami devastates Thailand's Andaman coast, killing thousands including many foreign tourists.
A military coup ousts Prime Minister Thaksin Shinawatra, deepening political polarization.
Yingluck Shinawatra becomes Thailand's first female prime minister amid continued political division.
The military seizes power in a coup led by General Prayut Chan-o-cha, establishing a junta.
King Bhumibol Adulyadej dies after a 70-year reign and is succeeded by his son, King Vajiralongkorn (Rama X).
Mass youth-led pro-democracy protests demand constitutional reform and unprecedented changes to the monarchy.
The progressive Move Forward Party wins the most seats in elections, but is blocked from forming a government.
Thailand legalizes same-sex marriage, becoming the first country in Southeast Asia to do so.
